nodes.label: Stage-2 Architecture — EGGROLL-PPCA (Perturbation-Pair Crossbar Array)
nodes.summary: Closes Stage-2 learning-rule question from formulation v1 by committing to score-function/ES (EGGROLL) realized as a dual-crossbar (slow G + fast rank-1 ab^T) analog architecture with Gilbert-cell consolidation and subthreshold fitness. Forward-only, all-analog critical path; natural batch size K_batch = τ_slow/τ_fast falls out of device physics. Structural hub unifying five communities.

nodes.label: Phase 1 — Perturbation Draw (counter-RNG a,b ~ N(0,σ²))
nodes.summary: Counter-based deterministic RNG produces reproducible a ∈ ℝ^m and b ∈ ℝ^n; written as analog voltages onto the perturbation crossbar rows/columns. Regeneratable at consolidation without storage.

nodes.label: Phase 2 — Combined Forward Pass (y = Gx + α·a·(b·x))
nodes.summary: Input x broadcast to both crossbars. Base crossbar emits Gx via Kirchhoff; perturbation crossbar emits a·(b·x) as a rank-1 structured product. Output summed at current node. One crossbar latency + one rank-1 broadcast — not two MVMs.
nodes.norm_label: phase 2 — combined forward pass (y = gx + α·a·(b·x))

nodes.label: Phase 3 — Nonlinearity N_α via Kink-Biased MOSFETs
nodes.summary: Column of kink-biased MOSFETs implements piecewise-linear N_kink activation (formulation v1 Option α). Activation function is fixed by kink/punch-through I–V device physics, not software.
nodes.norm_label: phase 3 — nonlinearity n_α via kink-biased mosfets

nodes.label: Phase 4 — Inter-Layer Analog Routing (no intermediate digitization)
nodes.summary: Intermediate layer outputs z stay analog and feed the next PPCA layer's x directly. D comparator is only invoked at final output — minimizes ADC overhead. Relies on shared-silicon claim: final-layer MOSFETs dual-role as N+D, earlier layers only N.
nodes.norm_label: phase 4 — inter-layer analog routing (no intermediate digitization)

nodes.label: Phase 5 — Fitness Scoring S = exp(−||z−t||²/2σ²) (single subthreshold transistor)
nodes.summary: Gaussian-approximate EGGROLL score realized in a single subthreshold-exponential transistor; outputs scalar S as a current level on a reference wire.
nodes.norm_label: phase 5 — fitness scoring s = exp(−||z−t||2/2σ2) (single subthreshold transistor)

nodes.label: Phase 6 — Consolidation ΔG_ij = η·S·a_i·b_j via Gilbert-Cell Strip
nodes.summary: Strip of Gilbert cells evaluates four-quadrant S·a_i·b_j; currents program charge-trap cells of the base crossbar. Slow (μs) programming acts as analog low-pass filter over many fast Phase 1–5 iterations; no digital accumulator needed.
nodes.norm_label: phase 6 — consolidation δg_ij = η·s·a_i·b_j via gilbert-cell strip

nodes.label: Lever 1 — Tiling (N×N → (N/k)² tiles)
nodes.summary: Logical N×N crossbar implemented as (N/k)² physical tiles (k≈256). Independent during Phase 2 (no horizontal dependency in Kirchhoff summation). Tile pipelining. Latency O(log(N/k)), area O(N²).

nodes.label: Lever 2 — Rank-r Perturbation with Precision-for-Rank Tradeoff
nodes.summary: EGGROLL perturbation is rank-r ≪ min(m,n). Write bandwidth O(r(m+n)) not O(mn). Pair-3 identity r ≤ b·redundancy links cell bit-depth to usable rank: a hardware design knob, not an algorithmic one.
nodes.norm_label: lever 2 — rank-r perturbation with precision-for-rank tradeoff

nodes.label: Lever 3 — Two-Timescale Pipelining (τ_slow ≈ 10 μs, τ_fast ≈ 100 ns)
nodes.summary: Charge-trap programming τ_slow ≈ 10 μs; floating-bulk perturbation and forward/fitness ≈ 100 ns. Natural pipelining of fast inner loop against slow consolidation.
nodes.norm_label: lever 3 — two-timescale pipelining (τ_slow ≈ 10 μs, τ_fast ≈ 100 ns)

nodes.label: Timescale Identity K_batch = τ_slow / (τ_fast_perturb + τ_forward + τ_fitness) ≈ 50
nodes.summary: The natural number of perturbations per consolidation is derived from device timescales, not chosen. ≈ 10 μs / 200 ns ≈ 50, which matches typical EGGROLL K. Falls out of physics; ties device property to algorithmic hyperparameter.
nodes.norm_label: timescale identity k_batch = τ_slow / (τ_fast_perturb + τ_forward + τ_fitness) ≈ 50

nodes.label: Stage-2 Commitment to Score-Function / ES (EGGROLL)
nodes.summary: Of formulation v1's three Stage-2 options, ES is chosen because its five required operations (perturbation draw, forward pass, fitness, outer-product update, consolidation) all already have device-native realizations in the graph. The other two options add teacher wiring or a backward path.

nodes.label: Rejection — Layer-Local (NoProp / Hebbian + Global Signal)
nodes.summary: Layer-local Stage-2 option rejected: requires per-layer teacher wiring, breaks forward-only purity, adds per-layer area cost. Documented as explicit graph-level incompatibility so future additions do not silently reopen Stage-2.

nodes.label: Rejection — Surrogate / Straight-Through Estimator
nodes.summary: Surrogate-gradient Stage-2 option rejected: requires an explicit backward path and ~2× silicon area; violates forward-only all-analog critical-path requirement.

nodes.label: PPCA Structural Hub — Five-Community God-Node Candidate
nodes.summary: The architecture's neighbors span five distinct communities: Analog Multiplication Family (W, Gilbert), Subthreshold Nonlinearity (N, fitness), Two-Timescale Bridge (charge-trap, floating-bulk), Bit-Level Physics (encoder), Local/Non-Backprop Learning (ES). High betweenness by construction.
nodes.norm_label: ppca structural hub — five-community god-node candidate

nodes.label: Dual-Crossbar Topology — Base G (charge-trap) + Perturbation a·b^T (floating-bulk)
nodes.summary: Two co-located crossbars: the slow parameter G uses charge-trap long-term retention; the fast rank-1 perturbation α·ab^T uses floating-bulk short-term plasticity. Gilbert-cell strip at bottom consolidates.
nodes.norm_label: dual-crossbar topology — base g (charge-trap) + perturbation a·b^t (floating-bulk)

nodes.label: Shared-Silicon Dual Role — Final-Layer MOSFETs Serve N and D
nodes.summary: The D comparator array reuses the same MOSFET species at different bias. Only the final layer's MOSFETs serve dual-role as N+D; earlier layers' MOSFETs serve only as N. Instantiates formulation v1's shared_silicon_claim.
nodes.norm_label: shared-silicon dual role — final-layer mosfets serve n and d

nodes.label: Forward-Only All-Analog Critical Path

nodes.label: A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Noorizadegan, Wang, Ling (2025). Systematic review of KAN architecture: basis function choices (B-splines, Chebyshev, Jacobi, RBF, Fourier, wavelet, sinc), KAT-to-KAN translation, bridging KANs and MLPs, accuracy/efficiency/regularization improvements, a Choose-Your-KAN practical guide.
nodes.norm_label: a practitioner's guide to kolmogorov-arnold networks

nodes.label: Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem (KAT)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Every continuous f: [0,1]^n -> R admits f(x_1,...,x_n) = sum_{q=0}^{2n} Phi_q ( sum_{p=1}^n phi_{q,p}(x_p) ) with continuous univariate inner maps phi_{q,p}: [0,1] -> R and outer maps Phi_q: R -> R. Inner maps can be chosen universal (independent of f); outer maps carry f-dependence. Lorentz and Sprecher refinements give simpler forms (single outer Phi, single inner phi with shifts eta_q and offsets c_q).

nodes.label: Learnable univariate activations on edges (vs fixed MLP node activations)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3 'From KAT to KAN' Eq. (6), Section 4 'Bridging KANs and MLPs'
nodes.summary: KAN layer: x_q^{(l+1)} = sum_{p=1}^P phi_{q,p}^{(l)}( x_p^{(l)} ). Each edge (p,q) carries its own learnable 1D nonlinearity; no linear mix before activation. MLP is mix-then-activate with fixed sigma; KAN is activate-then-sum. This reversal yields parameter efficiency: KAN depth L width W grid G costs O(G W^2 L) vs equivalent MLP's O(G^2 W^4 L).
nodes.norm_label: learnable univariate activations on edges (vs fixed mlp node activations)

nodes.label: B-spline basis parameterization per edge
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.summary: Standard KAN parameterizes each edge as phi_{q,p}^{(l)}(x) = sum_{k=1}^K c_{q,p,k}^{(l)} B_k(x) with learnable coefficients c_{q,p,k} and fixed B-spline basis {B_k}. Spline locality+smoothness gives compact, interpretable per-edge 1D transforms. Other bases (Chebyshev, Jacobi, Gaussian RBF, Fourier, ReLU^k, wavelet, sinc) can be substituted without changing (6).

nodes.label: Trainability via smooth differentiable basis (modernizes pathological KAT)
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3 'Remark' p.6, and Section 3 'Note that the exact inner functions constructed in KAT are often pathological'
nodes.summary: Classical KAT constructions used highly irregular (fractal-like, non-Lipschitz) inner functions, which made the depth-2 width-(2n+1) realization hard to optimize. Modern KANs dodge this by replacing exact KAT maps with smooth learnable parameterizations (splines / polynomials / RBFs), enabling end-to-end gradient training.
nodes.norm_label: trainability via smooth differentiable basis (modernizes pathological kat)

nodes.label: Claimed advantages: interpretability, parameter efficiency, sample/convergence gains
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 4 'Bridging KANs and MLPs' summary p.7, Table 2 pp.3-4
nodes.summary: KAN literature claims: (i) interpretability via inspectable per-edge 1D maps; (ii) parameter count O(GW^2L) vs MLP O(G^2W^4L); (iii) often faster convergence on PDE / regression tasks despite slower per-iteration cost; (iv) better accuracy for symbolic and physics-informed regimes when basis matches problem structure. Authors caution gains depend strongly on basis choice and are not monolithic.
nodes.norm_label: claimed advantages: interpretability, parameter efficiency, sample/convergence gains

nodes.label: Why KAN edge-functions and exact invariances are disjoint topics
nodes.source_file: raw/A Practitioner's Guide to Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks.pdf
nodes.source_location: KAN paper: grep for 'invariance'/'equivariance'/'group' returns only Section 5 SPEL-KAN Lie-symmetry extension mention; Invariances paper grep for 'Kolmogorov'/'KAN' returns zero hits
nodes.summary: Body-read resolves the prior AMBIGUOUS KAN<->Invariances edge. The KAN survey is about 1D basis parameterizations on edges (B-splines, Chebyshev, Fourier) of a feedforward decomposition f = sum_q Phi_q(sum_p phi_{qp}(x_p)); it does not address group-invariant function classes (only one passing reference to SPEL-KAN with Lie symmetries). The Invariances paper is about kernel regression on Riemannian manifolds under finite group G-actions via Laplace-Beltrami eigen-decomposition; it never references Kolmogorov-Arnold or KANs. The two methods live on different axes: architecture parameterization vs statistical-computational trade-offs of symmetry.
nodes.norm_label: why kan edge-functions and exact invariances are disjoint topics

nodes.label: CMOS-integrated Organic Neuromorphic Imagers for Dual-Modal Imaging
nodes.summary: A 640x512-pixel CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imager that operates in two modes: standard frame-based imaging and synaptic neuromorphic imaging. The synaptic mode extracts high-resolution spatiotemporal maps (light distribution and motion trajectories) from final frames by decoding temporal sequences of light events through contrast analysis. Adjustable memory (delay 10^1 to 10^4 ms) via modulation of charge recombination-trapping dynamics; retention >18 min. Demonstrates potential for industrial monitoring and motion detection.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: cmos-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for dual-modal imaging

nodes.label: Hybrid CMOS + organic BHJ device stack
nodes.summary: Lateral two-terminal organic neuromorphic device with donor-acceptor bulk heterojunctions (BHJ) of PTB7-Th and PZ1 between Au electrodes, with (3-Mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane (MPTS) as an interfacial trap layer. Without MPTS, memory effects are negligible. The BHJ + trap stack is co-integrated with a readout integrated circuit (ROIC) via a CMOS-compatible photolithography method using photocrosslinking polymers, enabling non-destructive patterning down to ~1 um and monolithic integration of organic synaptic pixels on CMOS.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: hybrid cmos + organic bhj device stack

nodes.label: Dual-modal operation: static frame + dynamic synaptic
nodes.summary: The imager operates in two modes. Standard mode: conventional frame-based static image capture via the ROIC. Synaptic mode: the organic device's photonic non-volatility (STP->LTP transition, paired-pulse facilitation, retention >18 min) accumulates light events so that a single final frame encodes the motion trajectory / light-event distribution map over tens of minutes. Dynamic information is preprocessed inside the pixel, eliminating the need to store/transmit every frame.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: dual-modal operation: static frame + dynamic synaptic

nodes.label: Charge recombination-trapping dynamics as tunable memory mechanism
nodes.summary: The learning/memory mechanism is physical rather than algorithmic: photogenerated electrons trapped at the MPTS interface layer while holes flow, leading to a sublinear EPSC rise/decay. The decay time constant tau_0 (time to 1/e of peak EPSC) is tuned by the weight percentage of PZ1, which modifies the density of recombination centers. This gives adjustable delay from 10^1 to 10^4 ms, STP->LTP transitions, photonic writing and electrical erasing at -5 V, and energy of ~4 fJ per event at 0.2 V / 5 ms.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: charge recombination-trapping dynamics as tunable memory mechanism

nodes.label: CMOS-compatible photocrosslinking patterning for organic BHJs
nodes.summary: A photolithography method that uses photocrosslinking between polymers to pattern organic bulk heterojunctions non-destructively at ~1 um resolution, the key enabling step for integrating organic neuromorphic devices with wafer-scale CMOS readout at 640x512 resolution. Prior organic neuromorphic devices could not be patterned without damage, which had previously blocked high-resolution dual-modal imagers.
nodes.source_file: raw/CMOS-integrated organic neuromorphic imagers for high-resolution dual-modal imaging.pdf
nodes.norm_label: cmos-compatible photocrosslinking patterning for organic bhjs

nodes.label: TEXEL: Mixed-Signal Neuromorphic Processor for Beyond-CMOS Integration
nodes.summary: TEXEL is a fabricated mixed-signal neuromorphic SNN processor (Greatorex et al., Nat. Commun. 2025) that combines subthreshold-CMOS spiking neurons and analog plastic synapses with a Back-End-of-Line (BEOL) device-agnostic differential synaptic interface for ~10K plastic synapses. The interface supports a wide range of two- and three-terminal memristive devices atop the CMOS chip, serving as a platform to test bio-inspired on-chip learning alongside emerging beyond-CMOS devices. Network-level experiments run Vector-Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) on-chip.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: texel: mixed-signal neuromorphic processor for beyond-cmos integration

nodes.label: BiCaLL on-chip local learning rule (STDP + Hebbian/SRDP)
nodes.summary: On-chip plasticity is implemented with mixed-signal circuitry embedded in each plastic synapse that emulates the Bistable Calcium-based Local Learning (BiCaLL) rule: STDP dominates at low activity (good for temporal pattern recognition with sparse spikes), Hebbian/Spike-Rate-Dependent Plasticity (SRDP) dominates at high activity (good for rate-based learning and evidence accumulation). A slower postsynaptic Ca2+ trace acts as a plasticity-gating signal that restricts updates to specific firing-rate ranges.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: bicall on-chip local learning rule (stdp + hebbian/srdp)

nodes.label: Bistable binary plastic synapse with analog internal weight
nodes.summary: Each plastic synapse quantizes to a binary efficacy but maintains a bistable analog internal weight V_w. Bistability circuitry pushes V_w toward one of two attractor values after accumulated updates, mitigating catastrophic forgetting in binary synapses. The on-chip weight matrix can be read out after learning and programmed for inference. This addresses the classical trade-off in pure-CMOS neuromorphic systems between silicon-area cost of storing analog weights and stability of long-term memory.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: bistable binary plastic synapse with analog internal weight

nodes.label: BEOL differential interface for two- and three-terminal memristors
nodes.summary: Device class: the chip is not wedded to one memristor type; its BEOL differential synaptic interface supports two- and three-terminal memristive devices (e.g. Valence Change Mechanism (VCM) cells are simulated, plus phase-change and ferroelectric candidates from collaborating groups) integrated atop the CMOS. The differential interface enables high-frequency, temporally unstructured reprogramming (needed for real-time on-chip learning) while retaining weight-read functionality. Scale: ~10K plastic synapses in a single chip, an order-of-magnitude step beyond prior test-structure demonstrations.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf
nodes.norm_label: beol differential interface for two- and three-terminal memristors

nodes.label: Subthreshold analog + event-driven asynchronous architecture
nodes.summary: TEXEL exploits the subthreshold-CMOS regime for neuron and synapse dynamics combined with asynchronous event-based digital routing (spikes are digital events). This yields ultra-low-power SNN operation suited to edge applications like bio-signal and environmental monitoring. Power measurements per circuit block quantify the efficiency gains from subthreshold analog + event-driven signaling, establishing a baseline for future fully-memristive variants.
nodes.source_file: raw/A neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning for beyond-CMOS device integration.pdf

nodes.label: Formulation Layer D — Deterministic Dynamics and Theorems 1-3
nodes.summary: Layer D specifies the deterministic coupled-ODE dynamics (D1–D6) of the device state given Layer C's symbolic foundation, and proves three theorems that together establish physics-closed signal-processing completeness: (T1) well-posedness, (T2) operator-level realizability, (T3) input-to-output map is a switched time-varying Volterra operator. Stochastic effects are deferred to Layer S.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: formulation layer d — deterministic dynamics and theorems 1-3

nodes.label: ODE (D1) — Fast activation dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_x · ẋ(t) = −x(t) + N(W(t)·E[u](t) + δW(t)·E[u](t)). Fast activation dynamics: x(t) is the RC-filtered response of the nonlinearity output.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D2) — Perturbation reset dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_δ · δẆ(t) = −δW(t) + Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤ · δ(t − t_k). δW(t) is a leaky integrator of impulsive rank-1 resets arriving at perturbation event times {t_k}.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D3) — Consolidation dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_W · Ẇ(t) = −W(t) + η · S(x(t), y*(t)) · δW(t). Slow weight W drifts under fitness-weighted perturbation accumulation.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D4) — Archive snapshot event
nodes.summary: W̄(t_k⁺) = W(t_k) at snapshot events t_k. Discrete-time jump decoupled from the continuous dynamics; archives current slow weight.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D5) — Decoder readout
nodes.summary: y(t) = D(x(t)). Instantaneous decoder readout of activation state — comparator array applied to x.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D6) — Sensor readout
nodes.summary: s(t) = R(W(t)). On-demand sensor readout of the slow weight matrix via sense-amp with row-column select.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 — Well-Posedness of (D1)–(D3)
nodes.summary: For bandlimited finite-energy u, Lipschitz N on operating range, continuous target y*, and specified initial conditions, system (D1)–(D3) has a unique absolutely continuous solution (x(·), W(·), δW(·)) on ℝ₊, with W(t) ∈ 𝒲 for all t > 0.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 1: piecewise classical between impulses
nodes.summary: Between impulse arrivals [t_{k-1}, t_k), (D2) reduces to τ_δ · δẆ = −δW with closed-form decay δW(t) = δW(t_{k-1}⁺) · exp(−(t − t_{k-1})/τ_δ). (D1) and (D3) are classical ODEs on each inter-arrival interval.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1 proof — step 1: piecewise classical between impulses

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 2: Lipschitz on compact state
nodes.summary: f_x(x,W,δW,t) is Lipschitz in (x,W,δW) uniformly in t on compact sub-intervals, since N is Lipschitz and W, δW are bounded. Picard–Lindelöf gives existence and uniqueness on each sub-interval.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1 proof — step 2: lipschitz on compact state

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 3: W stays bounded (feasibility condition)
nodes.summary: ‖(−W + η S δW)/τ_W‖ ≤ (W_max + η·σ·r)/τ_W since S ≤ 1 and ‖δW‖ ≤ σ²√(nm). Combined with −W/τ_W decay, W(t) ∈ 𝒲 for all t > 0 provided W_max > η σ² √(nm) (feasibility condition).
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1 proof — step 3: w stays bounded (feasibility condition)

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 4: impulse concatenation and uniqueness
nodes.summary: At t = t_k, δW has a finite jump δW(t_k⁺) = δW(t_k⁻) + a_k b_k^⊤; (x,W) continues continuously. Concatenating piecewise-classical solutions gives a global absolutely continuous solution on ℝ₊ with δW having countable jump discontinuities. Uniqueness follows from Picard–Lindelöf per sub-interval plus deterministic jump rule.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1 proof — step 4: impulse concatenation and uniqueness

nodes.label: Theorem 1 remark — Lipschitz constants for three canonical N
nodes.summary: f_kink Lipschitz with constant max(α, α+β); f_sat linear Lipschitz in saturation region; f_sub grows unboundedly and requires explicit domain truncation to [V_SS, V_DD] (physically automatic from silicon rails). Theorem 1 is therefore not trivial for f_sub.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 1 remark — lipschitz constants for three canonical n

nodes.label: Theorem 2 — Operator-Level Realizability
nodes.summary: Every operator appearing in (D1)–(D6) admits an explicit mapping to a CMOS-compatible device primitive. No step in the dynamics requires a computation outside the Layer C operator set. Proof is a constructive mapping table.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 2 proof — Constructive device-realization mapping table
nodes.summary: Table mapping each term in (D1)–(D6) to a CMOS primitive and Layer C definition: E[u]→anti-alias RC capacitor, W·v→Kirchhoff crossbar sum, N→transistor-regime stage, RC filters→node capacitor integrators, Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤ δ(t-t_k)→counter-RNG pulse + Gilbert-cell outer product, S→diff-amp+squarer+subthreshold exp, D→comparator array, R→sense-amp, Σ/Π→flash/MRAM program+read.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 2 proof — constructive device-realization mapping table

nodes.label: Key observation — Derivatives are inverse of RC integrator (physics-closed)
nodes.summary: The three derivatives ẋ, δẆ, Ẇ are not separate operations but the inverse view of I_τ (the RC leaky integrator). An RC filter IS a leaky integrator; differentiation is just the read of a voltage decaying through the RC. No step where a computer differentiates — this is what makes the formulation physics-closed.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: key observation — derivatives are inverse of rc integrator (physics-closed)

nodes.label: Theorem 3 — Signal-Processing Completeness (Switched Volterra Representation)
nodes.summary: Central theorem. With W(t) held slowly varying (τ_W ≫ τ_x), the input-output map T_W[u](t) := x(t) is (a) a classical Volterra series for analytic N, or (b) a switched Volterra operator partitioned into polytopes {P_q} for piecewise-linear N. Composition u → E → Volterra/switched Volterra → D (or R) is entirely a classical signal-processing chain — no symbolic, discrete, or lookup step intervenes.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 proof — Part (a): Volterra series for analytic N
nodes.summary: Taylor-expand N around bias z₀ = W·E[u](0); substitute into (D1); solve iteratively in powers of input amplitude (Rugh 1981). Kernels: h_1(t;τ;W) = h_x(t−τ)·W·h_E(τ), h_2(t;τ_1,τ_2;W) = (1/2)N''(z₀)·h_x(t−max(τ_1,τ_2))·(W h_E)(τ_1) ⊗ (W h_E)(τ_2); higher orders by recursion. Convergence requires ‖u‖ within a radius determined by N's analytic properties.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 3 proof — part (a): volterra series for analytic n

nodes.label: Theorem 3 proof — Part (b): switched Volterra for piecewise-linear N
nodes.summary: f_kink partitions ℝ into 3 linear regions by thresholds {V_T, V_k}. Region tuple R(t) ∈ {0,1,2}^n takes ≤ 3^n values. In each q, N_q(z) = A_q z + b_q and (D1) becomes an LTI system with kernel h_1^q(t;τ;W) = h_x(t−τ)·A_q·(W+δW)·h_E(τ). Global map is a switched linear Volterra operator with finitely many regions and analog threshold-crossing switching events. δW updates shift region boundaries at each perturbation event.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 3 proof — part (b): switched volterra for piecewise-linear n

nodes.label: Theorem 3 — Explicit Volterra kernel expressions
nodes.summary: Explicit closed-form expressions for Volterra kernels: 1st-order h_1 = h_x * W * h_E; 2nd-order h_2 involves N''(z₀) with outer-product input; in switched case h_1^q per region with gain A_q and bias b_q. Activation RC impulse response h_x(t) = (1/τ_x) e^{−t/τ_x} 1_{t ≥ 0}.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 remark — Formulation v1 Composition Theorem is a special case
nodes.summary: When E is the binarized encoder, N is the single-kink ReLU limit (V_k=∞), and D is sign, the switched Volterra representation collapses to a single region (sign-of-inner-product) and kernels reduce to XNOR+popcount MAC expressions. Theorem 3 is the general case; Formulation v1 is its specialization.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 3 remark — formulation v1 composition theorem is a special case

nodes.label: Section 5 claim — Euler discretization reduces (D1)–(D3) to NN + EGGROLL
nodes.summary: Under uniform time discretization Δt and Euler approximation, (D1)–(D3) reduces to standard layered NN forward pass plus EGGROLL update. Taking Δt = τ_x gives x_{t+Δt} ≈ N(W·E(u_t)) (one-layer forward pass); stacking layers gives multi-layer forward. Consolidation discretized at Δt = τ_W/K_batch gives W_{t+Δt} ≈ (1 − 1/K_batch) W_t + (η/K_batch)·S_t·δW_t — the exact EGGROLL rank-1 update. Upgrades K_batch = τ_W/τ_δ ≈ 50 from motif to formal correspondence.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: section 5 claim — euler discretization reduces (d1)–(d3) to nn + eggroll

nodes.label: AMBIGUOUS-resolution Edge 1 — Punch-Through ↔ Depth as Iterative Operator Flow
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 resolves AMBIGUOUS Edge 1: 'Depth as iterative operator flow' is precisely the composition of per-layer switched Volterra operators. Punch-through provides the threshold that induces region switching. Composition-of-operators (discrete depth) = iteration of the switched Volterra flow (continuous time). Upgrade EXTRACTED 1.0.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: ambiguous-resolution edge 1 — punch-through ↔ depth as iterative operator flow

nodes.label: AMBIGUOUS-resolution Edge 2 — Kink Effect ↔ State-Region Dictionary
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 resolves AMBIGUOUS Edge 2: 'State-region dictionary' is the partition {P_q} of input space; each region corresponds to a specific region-tuple R(t) ∈ {0,1,2}^n. Kink effect provides the per-unit threshold that defines the partition boundaries. Upgrade EXTRACTED 1.0.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: ambiguous-resolution edge 2 — kink effect ↔ state-region dictionary

nodes.label: Open point 1 — Bandwidth/convergence radius of the Volterra series
nodes.summary: The k-th kernel h_k has support width ~k·τ_x. For the series to represent bandlimited inputs faithfully, convergence requires ‖u‖_∞ ≤ u_max with radius dependent on N's analytic structure. Computable but tedious; deferred to Layer S where it also governs noise propagation.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open point 1 — bandwidth/convergence radius of the volterra series

nodes.label: Open point 2 — Uniform consolidation (W slowly varying) assumption
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 holds W constant on the τ_x timescale. Justified by τ_W ≫ τ_x but becomes quantitative only with Layer S noise model. Rigorous proof: perturbation analysis W(t) = W_0 + δ(t) with ‖δ‖ = O(τ_x/τ_W).
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open point 2 — uniform consolidation (w slowly varying) assumption

nodes.label: Open point 3 — Switching-event dynamics at region transitions
nodes.summary: Theorem 3(b) glosses over exact dynamics at region transitions. Measure-zero events for generic inputs but may matter under worst-case adversarial input. Deferred.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open point 3 — switching-event dynamics at region transitions

nodes.label: Open point 4 — Multi-layer region count (Zaslavsky / Hanin-Rolnick)
nodes.summary: A multi-layer device is a cascade of switched Volterra operators, each with its own region partition. Global partition cardinality grows as 3^{Σ n_ℓ}. Connects to State-Region Dictionary's region-count theorem — a Zaslavsky-type hyperplane arrangement bound. Left open until Stage 2 incorporates Hanin-Rolnick.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open point 4 — multi-layer region count (zaslavsky / hanin-rolnick)

nodes.label: Formulation Layer S — Stochastic Dynamics and Theorems 4-5
nodes.summary: Layer S extends Layer D's deterministic ODE system (D1-D3) into a coupled SDE system (S1-S3) augmented with four separable device-noise components (thermal, 1/f flicker, device-to-device variability, perturbation-clock jitter). Two theorems are proved on this SDE: Theorem 4 (almost-sure convergence of slow-weight dynamics to a local minimum of the fitness landscape under Robbins-Monro schedule and two-timescale averaging) and Theorem 5 (explicit steady-state weight-error bound as an additive function of device-parameter-indexed noise variances divided by effective learning rate and λ_min(H)). A corollary asserts that the resulting three-tier memory device performs continual learning under data-distribution shift with arbitrary task-interruption tolerance. Section 7 claims formulation completeness (T1-T5 + corollary = existence, realizability, completeness, convergence, noise, archive).
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: formulation layer s — stochastic dynamics and theorems 4-5

nodes.label: Thermal (kT/C) white noise — σ_th² = k_B T / C_node
nodes.summary: White Gaussian kT/C noise on every capacitive node, fully uncorrelated across cells and time. For charge-trap-site τ_W capacitors at pF scale, thermal voltage noise is ~μV-mV. Appears as σ_x dB^x, σ_δ dB^δ, σ_W dB^W Brownian-motion terms in S1/S2/S3. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_th² = σ_W² + σ_x²·(gain_fwd)² to the bound.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: thermal (kt/c) white noise — σ_th2 = k_b t / c_node

nodes.label: Flicker (1/f) noise — S(ω) = K_f / |ω|^α, α≈1
nodes.summary: Time-correlated 1/f^α noise present on every MOS transistor, dominant in the subthreshold regime that N_β uses for the Gaussian score primitive. Not reducible to Brownian motion; properly modeled by fractional Brownian motion or a 1/f-noise SDE. Enters S1 as (1/f)·dF^x(t). In Theorem 5 contributes σ_flicker²·τ_f, where τ_f ≈ τ_W is the spectral weight of 1/f at the slow-weight timescale. Long-memory behavior at timescales > τ_W is an open problem (§8.1) that could break the OU stationary assumption.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Device-to-device variability — static multiplicative+additive mismatch
nodes.summary: Static but random per-cell offsets: ΔV_T, tail-current mismatch in Gilbert pairs, charge-trap-depth variations. Constant in time post-fabrication but random across the array. Modeled as W_ij^eff = W_ij·(1+ε_ij^mul) + ε_ij^add with ε^mul, ε^add drawn once from fabrication distributions. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_variability² = Var(ε^mul)·‖W*‖² + Var(ε^add).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Perturbation-clock jitter — Poisson event-time noise on ξ
nodes.summary: The counter-RNG produces quasi-deterministic event times (same per seed), but any physical oscillator adds jitter Δt_k ~ 𝒩(0, σ_jitter²). For EGGROLL analysis this appears as a variance in event-count per epoch. In Theorem 5 contributes σ_jitter² = σ_t²·‖∂S/∂t‖² — time-jitter noise propagated through the derivative of the score.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: perturbation-clock jitter — poisson event-time noise on ξ

nodes.label: SDE (S1) — activation dynamics with thermal + flicker noise
nodes.summary: dx(t) = (1/τ_x)·[−x + N(W E[u] + δW E[u])] dt + σ_x dB^x(t) + (1/f)·dF^x(t). Noise-augmented form of ODE (D1): fast activation x tracks the nonlinear combination of weights and input with exponential relaxation τ_x, plus white thermal Brownian motion and 1/f fractional contribution. Independent of S2/S3 Brownian motions.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: sde (s1) — activation dynamics with thermal + flicker noise

nodes.label: SDE (S2) — rank-1 perturbation δW with marked Poisson drive
nodes.summary: dδW(t) = (1/τ_δ)·[−δW] dt + Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤·dN_k(t) + σ_δ dB^δ(t). Perturbation state decays with short τ_δ (leaky integrator) but is periodically kicked by a marked Poisson process N_k with rank-1 marks (a_k, b_k), plus floating-bulk thermal Brownian. In the two-timescale limit τ_W ≫ τ_δ, δW reaches its stationary distribution under the perturbation arrivals before W moves.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: sde (s2) — rank-1 perturbation δw with marked poisson drive

nodes.label: SDE (S3) — slow weight W consolidation with program-write jumps
nodes.summary: dW(t) = (1/τ_W)·[−W + η·S(x, y*)·δW] dt + σ_W dB^W(t) + σ_W^prog·sgn(S δW) dN_prog(t). Slow W evolves on τ_W ≫ τ_δ under fitness-gated consolidation from δW, charge-trap thermal Brownian, and a second programming-pulse Poisson process adding write-noise jumps. Box-constrained to 𝒲 via reflection at saturation boundaries.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: sde (s3) — slow weight w consolidation with program-write jumps

nodes.label: ES score-function identity: averaged S3 drift = ∇_W log E[S]
nodes.summary: E_{(a_k,b_k)}[η·S(x,y*)·δW] = η·σ²·∇_W log E_{(a,b)}[S(x(W+ab^⊤), y*)] − η·σ²·∇_W log Z. The Williams-1992 / Wierstra-2014 score-function identity: the averaged rank-1 perturbation update in (S3), taken over the Gaussian perturbation distribution 𝒩(0,σ²), equals a scaled gradient of log-expected-score. ∇log Z vanishes for normalized scores. Conclusion: in the averaged drift dW_avg/dt ≈ η·σ²·∇_W E[S] + noise — EGGROLL's rank-1 perturbation update IS a score-function gradient estimator.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: es score-function identity: averaged s3 drift = ∇_w log e[s]

nodes.label: Two-timescale averaging condition τ_W ≫ τ_δ
nodes.summary: Required condition for the ES-score-estimator identity and Theorem 4 Step 1: the slow weight W sees an average over many perturbation marks because its timescale τ_W greatly exceeds the perturbation timescale τ_δ. Formally the condition that Borkar-2008 two-timescale stochastic approximation applies: fast δW reaches stationary distribution on the slow W's timescale. This formalizes Pair 6 (identity η·τ_W/τ_δ).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 4 — Learning convergence of W(t) to a local minimum W* a.s.
nodes.summary: Let u(t) be ergodic with stationary p_data, y*(u) measurable, L(W) := E_u[−S(x(W;u), y*(u))] have isolated local minima with non-degenerate Hessian, η_t = η_0/(1+t/T_0) be Robbins-Monro, and σ_x, σ_δ, σ_W < ∞. Then as t→∞, W(t) converges almost surely to a local minimum W* of L, with liminf ‖W(t)−W*‖ = 0 in any local basin. Does NOT claim global convergence (like any first-order method, ES gradient flow only reaches local minima). The physical τ_W realizes the η_t schedule: larger τ_W = smaller effective learning rate.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theorem 4 — learning convergence of w(t) to a local minimum w* a.s.

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 1 — Borkar two-timescale reduction to effective slow SDE
nodes.summary: Borkar-2008 two-timescale stochastic approximation: since τ_W ≫ τ_δ, the fast δW reaches stationary distribution on the timescale of slow W. Averaging (S3) over the fast process replaces δW(t) by its sample mean, yielding the effective slow SDE dW(t) = η(t)·∇_W L(W(t)) dt + σ_avg·dB(t), where σ_avg² aggregates all noise sources (thermal, flicker, write noise) through the averaging operation.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t4 proof step 1 — borkar two-timescale reduction to effective slow sde

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 2 — Lyapunov V(W)=L(W)−L(W*) with Itô's formula
nodes.summary: Take V(W) = L(W) − L(W*) as the candidate Lyapunov function in a local basin around W*. Itô's formula gives dV(W) = −η(t)‖∇L(W)‖² dt + η(t)·(1/2)·tr(σ² H_V(W)) dt + (stochastic part), where H_V is the Hessian. The deterministic drift is strictly negative away from W*; the diffusion term is controlled by σ².
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t4 proof step 2 — lyapunov v(w)=l(w)−l(w*) with ito's formula

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 3 — Robbins-Monro convergence via Kushner-Yin
nodes.summary: With η(t) Robbins-Monro (∫η dt = ∞, ∫η² dt < ∞), the stochastic-integral martingale term vanishes almost surely. The expected drift E[dV] = −η(t)·(‖∇L‖² − σ²·Tr(H_V)/2) dt is strictly negative near W* for small enough noise (where ‖∇L‖ bounded below by linearity). Classical stochastic approximation (Kushner-Yin 2003) gives V(W(t)) → 0 a.s., hence W(t) → W*.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t4 proof step 3 — robbins-monro convergence via kushner-yin

nodes.label: T4 Proof Step 4 — Box-constraint feasibility via reflecting boundary
nodes.summary: The W ∈ 𝒲 box constraint (from charge-trap physical saturation, Layer C Tier-2 state) is enforced as a reflecting barrier when the SDE trajectory hits the boundary — standard technique in constrained SDE analysis. Within the interior of 𝒲, Steps 1-3 apply. This closes the proof.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t4 proof step 4 — box-constraint feasibility via reflecting boundary

nodes.label: Theorem 5 — Noise tolerance: E[‖W(∞)−W*‖²] ≤ C_H·Σσ²/η
nodes.summary: In the stationary regime of (S1)-(S3) near a local minimum W* with Hessian H = ∇²L(W*) ≻ 0, the expected squared deviation satisfies E[‖W(∞)−W*‖²] ≤ C_H·(σ_th² + σ_flicker²·τ_f + σ_variability² + σ_jitter²)/η, with C_H = 1/λ_min(H). Additionally bias(E[W(∞)]) ≤ C_H·σ_ξ/r — the rank-r structure gives the O(r^{-1}) bias-reduction matching EGGROLL's rank convergence theorem. Answers 'what's the worst-case weight-error budget given device thermal X, 1/f Y, variability Z?' — budget set by λ_min(H): well-conditioned losses tolerate more noise, ill-conditioned require tighter control.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 1 — Linearize SDE near W* to Ornstein-Uhlenbeck
nodes.summary: Near a local minimum with full-rank Hessian H, the SDE linearizes to dW(t) = −η·H·(W−W*) dt + σ_eff·dB(t) — an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process with mean W* and covariance determined by H and σ_eff.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t5 proof step 1 — linearize sde near w* to ornstein-uhlenbeck

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 2 — OU stationary covariance via Lyapunov equation
nodes.summary: The stationary covariance Σ = E[(W−W*)(W−W*)^⊤] solves the Lyapunov equation η·H·Σ + η·Σ·H = σ_eff²·I, giving Σ = (σ_eff²/2η)·H^{-1}. Taking trace: E[‖W−W*‖²] = Tr(Σ) = (σ_eff²/2η)·Tr(H^{-1}) ≤ (σ_eff²/2η)·(d/λ_min(H)), where d = nm. Rearranging gives the C_H = 1/λ_min(H) form in Theorem 5.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t5 proof step 2 — ou stationary covariance via lyapunov equation

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 3 — Finite-rank-estimator bias ∝ 1/r
nodes.summary: The ES estimator is unbiased in expectation but has finite variance scaling as 1/r for rank-r (standard covariance-rank identity for ES, rank-r EGGROLL paper). Adding this bias to the OU-induced variance gives the full bound bias(E[W(∞)]) ≤ C_H·σ_ξ/r. This formally derives Pair 3 (cell bit-depth → usable rank → noise-floor): low-bit cells cap achievable rank, cap the bias reduction, set a noise floor below which further noise reduction doesn't help.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: T5 Proof Step 4 — Physical noise-source propagation into the bound
nodes.summary: Each σ² term arises from specific physical noise: (i) Thermal σ_th² = k_B T/C with C the smallest capacitor in the forward path; (ii) 1/f with τ_f the spectral weight at frequencies ~τ_W^{-1}; (iii) Variability σ_variability² = Var(ε^mul)·‖W*‖² + Var(ε^add); (iv) Jitter σ_jitter² = σ_t²·‖∂S/∂t‖² through the score derivative. All four contribute additively (independent noises). The bound is thus expressible entirely in device-physics parameters.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: t5 proof step 4 — physical noise-source propagation into the bound

nodes.label: Corollary — Continual learning via three-tier memory under distribution shift
nodes.summary: With Tier-3 snapshots triggered by learning-saturation (three_tier_memory_trigger_learning_saturation), archive W̄(t_k) = W(t_k) = W* + ε(t_k) with E[‖ε(t_k)‖²] ≤ Theorem-5 bound — a bias-plus-noise snapshot of the current local minimum. After restore, the device resumes from this bias; if distribution unchanged, restoration is noise-equivalent to one Theorem-5 sample; if shifted, adapts to the new minimum at rate 1/τ_W starting from stored W*. The semi-passive adaptive memory device can perform continual learning under data-distribution shift, with Tier-3 retention giving arbitrarily long task-interruption tolerance (power cycling) while Tier-2 dynamics handle online adaptation.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: corollary — continual learning via three-tier memory under distribution shift

nodes.label: Prediction 1 — Loss curves should match EGGROLL O(r^{-1}) convergence
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from Theorem 4 + rank analysis: empirical loss curves of the device-in-the-loop simulation should match EGGROLL's O(r^{-1}) rank-convergence scaling. Deviation indicates problems with either the device model (Layer C) or the dynamics (Layer D/S).
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: prediction 1 — loss curves should match eggroll o(r^{-1}) convergence

nodes.label: Prediction 2 — Steady-state weight MSE equals T5 bound with device σ's
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from Theorem 5: steady-state weight MSE in simulation should equal the Theorem 5 bound with substituted device σ's (σ_th, σ_flicker·√τ_f, σ_variability, σ_jitter). This is the falsifiable core test: if measured MSE exceeds the bound, either the noise model or Theorem 5 is wrong.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: prediction 2 — steady-state weight mse equals t5 bound with device σ's

nodes.label: Prediction 3 — Distribution-shift recovery on timescale τ_W
nodes.summary: Stage-2 prediction from §6 corollary: after a data-distribution shift, the device adapts to the new local minimum with an exponential approach timescale equal to τ_W (the slow-weight time constant). Deviation indicates Layer C/D/S revision needed.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: prediction 3 — distribution-shift recovery on timescale τ_w

nodes.label: Open problem 1 — 1/f long-memory effects beyond τ_W
nodes.summary: Theorem 5 assumed 1/f contribution integrable over τ_W. If 1/f dominates at timescales > τ_W, the OU stationary assumption (T5 Step 2) fails — in the long run W may exhibit non-stationary drift that Theorem 5 doesn't capture. Expected open until experimental Stage-3-5 data.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open problem 1 — 1/f long-memory effects beyond τ_w

nodes.label: Open problem 2 — Non-Gaussian perturbation distributions bias gradient estimator
nodes.summary: Theorem 4 relied on Gaussian perturbation for the score-function identity. If the counter-RNG produces sub-Gaussian or heavy-tailed distributions, the gradient estimator is biased. This is a hardware design constraint on the RNG block — RNG must produce approximately Gaussian marks.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open problem 2 — non-gaussian perturbation distributions bias gradient estimator

nodes.label: Open problem 3 — Correlated noise across cells (supply, substrate, bias)
nodes.summary: Power-supply noise, substrate coupling, shared bias lines introduce correlations between σ_W components across cells that Theorem 5 modeled as independent. Correcting gives a d term instead of Tr(H^{-1}) in the bound — possibly much larger for large arrays. Corresponds to Stage-3 cross-tile correlation problem from earlier PPCA note.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: open problem 3 — correlated noise across cells (supply, substrate, bias)

nodes.label: Formulation-complete check: T1+T2+T3+T4+T5+corollary
nodes.summary: Section 7 claim: after Layer S Theorems 4 and 5, six check-marks assert the mathematical formulation is complete — well-posedness (T1), operator realizability (T2), signal-processing completeness as switched Volterra operator (T3), learning convergence a.s. (T4), noise tolerance bound (T5), three-tier memory continual-learning corollary (§6). Every question (existence, realizability, completeness, convergence, noise, archive) is answered, all answers expressed in device-physics parameters. Three concrete predictions for Stage-2 simulation listed; three open problems in Section 8.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theory Background 1 — ODE Foundations and Volterra Series
nodes.summary: Consolidates six classical mathematical tools used in Layer D proofs (Theorems 1–3): Picard-Lindelöf, Lipschitz continuity, Gronwall inequality, LTI/convolution theory, Taylor expansion, and Volterra series. Organizes them into a single logical chain: deterministic ODE solutions exist → linear theory applies → nonlinear generalization via Volterra.
nodes.norm_label: theory background 1 — ode foundations and volterra series

nodes.label: Picard-Lindelöf Theorem
nodes.summary: For IVP ẋ = f(t,x), x(t₀)=x₀: if f is continuous in t and Lipschitz in x, then a unique solution exists on an interval [t₀−δ, t₀+δ]. Proof via Banach fixed-point / contraction mapping on T[x](t) := x₀ + ∫ f(s,x(s))ds, using L·δ < 1. References: Coddington & Levinson (1955); Hartman (1982).

nodes.label: Lipschitz Continuity
nodes.summary: Condition ‖f(x)−f(y)‖ ≤ L·‖x−y‖. Weaker than differentiability (|x| is Lipschitz but not C¹). For our N options: kink N_α has L = max(α, α+β); subthreshold exp N_β has L = I_S·exp(V_max/nV_T)/(nV_T); saturation N_γ has L = 2μC_ox(W/L)(V_max−V_T). Silicon rail [V_SS, V_DD] auto-truncates domain, so all N are Lipschitz on bounded domain.

nodes.label: Gronwall Inequality
nodes.summary: If u(t) ≤ α + ∫_{t₀}^{t} β(s)u(s)ds with α ≥ 0, then u(t) ≤ α·exp(∫_{t₀}^{t} β(s)ds). Universal tool for suppressing exponential growth of ODE solutions; gives quantitative bound on 'trajectory stays inside 𝒲 box' argument in Layer D Thm 1 Step 3. Reference: Teschl, Ch. 2.

nodes.label: LTI System Theory and Convolution Representation
nodes.summary: Any LTI operator L:u→y is expressible as convolution y(t) = (h*u)(t) = ∫ h(t−τ)u(τ)dτ, where h = L[δ] is the impulse response. For RC integrator τẋ+x=u, impulse response is h(t) = (1/τ)·exp(−t/τ)·1_{t≥0}, i.e. a first-order low-pass filter of time constant τ. Provides the mathematical basis for 'integration as a physical operation'. References: Oppenheim & Willsky Ch. 2; Papoulis Ch. 6.
nodes.norm_label: lti system theory and convolution representation

nodes.label: Taylor Expansion for Analytic N
nodes.summary: For analytic N near z₀: N(z) = Σ_{k≥0} (1/k!)·N^{(k)}(z₀)·(z−z₀)^k with radius of convergence R = distance to nearest singularity. Substituted into (D1) in Layer D Thm 3 Part (a) to derive Volterra kernels h_k recursively. Standard language for distortion analysis: 1st-order=linear response, 2nd-order=harmonic distortion, 3rd-order=IM3.

nodes.label: Volterra Series — Canonical Representation of Nonlinear Systems
nodes.summary: Time-invariant nonlinear system y=F[u], sufficiently smooth ⟹ y(t) = h_0 + Σ_{k≥1} ∫…∫ h_k(τ_1,…,τ_k)·u(t−τ_1)⋯u(t−τ_k) dτ_1⋯dτ_k. h_1 is LTI impulse response; h_k is k-fold convolution kernel. Derivation: Taylor-expand N, substitute into (D1), series-expand in ε for u→εu, collect terms x_k. References: Volterra 1930; Wiener 1942; Rugh 1981 (standard); Schetzen 1980; Boyd-Chua 1985 (convergence).
nodes.norm_label: volterra series — canonical representation of nonlinear systems

nodes.label: Switched Volterra for Piecewise-Linear N
nodes.summary: When N is piecewise-linear (kink), Taylor expansion breaks at junction points. Instead partition input space into polytopes {P_q}_{q∈Q} (one per N-region tuple); on each P_q the system is LTI with its own kernels; region transitions are threshold crossings (analytically discontinuous but measure zero). This is the switched Volterra operator used in Layer D Thm 3 Part (b).

nodes.label: Connectivity Chain — Five Tools Form One Argument
nodes.summary: Lipschitz → (enables) Picard-Lindelöf → Layer D Thm 1 (solution existence); + Gronwall → boundedness; → LTI+convolution → Layer D Thm 2 (operator realizability); + Taylor → nonlinear extension; → Volterra series → Layer D Thm 3 (signal-processing completeness). Converges on one proposition: 'a nonlinear ODE system implemented in physical devices is a classical signal-processing object.' Layer S's stochastic additions presuppose this deterministic skeleton.
nodes.norm_label: connectivity chain — five tools form one argument

nodes.label: Math Theory 02 — Piecewise-Linear Systems & Hyperplane Combinatorics
nodes.summary: 이론 배경 2: 구분 선형 시스템과 초평면 배치 조합론. Layer D Theorem 3 Part (b) — piecewise-linear N(kink)에 대한 switched Volterra 표현 — 과 State-Region Dictionary 논문과의 연결에서 사용한 조합 기하학 도구들. 핵심 주장: ReLU/kink 같은 조각 선형 활성화가 입력 공간을 유한한 polytope로 분할한다는 사실의 수학적 구조. 세 축(PWL 구조 정의, 조합 분석 via Zaslavsky+Hanin-Rolnick, 신호처리 객체 switched Volterra)이 결합되어 '깊이 있는 ReLU 네트워크는 유한 조각 선형 필터로 해석될 수 있다'는 주장을 완성.
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: math theory 02 — piecewise-linear systems & hyperplane combinatorics

nodes.label: Piecewise-Linear System (PWL)
nodes.summary: 구분 선형 함수 f: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ는 입력 공간의 유한 분할 {P_1, ..., P_Q}에서 각 영역 위에 affine map f|_{P_q}(x) = A_q x + b_q로 표현되는 함수. 각 P_q는 닫힌 polytope (유한 개 선형 부등식 교집합). 인접 polytope 경계에서 연속이면 CPWL(continuous piecewise-linear). Switched system 이론에서는 영역 전환을 switching이라 부름. ReLU σ(z)=max(0,z)는 조각 affine이며 L-layer ReLU 네트워크에서는 총 영역 수가 깊이에 따라 급증. Layer C Definition 3.3의 f_kink(z) = α(z−V_T)₊ + β(z−V_k)·1_{z>V_k}는 V_T와 V_k를 경계로 세 조각 선형 (기울기 0, α, α+β). 참조: Liberzon 2003, Johansson 2003.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Hyperplane Arrangement
nodes.summary: ℝⁿ에서 유한 개 초평면 집합 𝒜 = {H_1, ..., H_m}. 각 초평면 H_i = {x : a_i·x = b_i}. 배치의 여집합 ℝⁿ∖⋃H_i는 유한 개 연결 성분(region)으로 분할됨. 핵심 질문: 영역 개수는 몇 개인가? 일반 위치(general position) 가정: 임의의 k개 초평면 교집합이 여차원 k를 가짐; 실제 ReLU 네트워크의 초평면은 거의 항상 이 조건을 측도 1 argument로 만족. 참조: Stanley (IAS/Park City 2004), Orlik & Terao (Springer 1992).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Zaslavsky's Theorem (Region Count Formula)
nodes.summary: Zaslavsky 1975: 일반 위치 ℝⁿ 속 m개 초평면 배치 𝒜의 영역 개수는 r(𝒜) = Σ_{k=0}^n C(m,k). 유도는 귀납법 + 삭제-수축(deletion-contraction) 공식 r(𝒜) = r(𝒜−H) + r(𝒜^H). 특수 경우: n=1, m점 → 영역 m+1; n=2, m직선 일반 위치 → 영역 1+m+C(m,2) = (m²+m+2)/2; n차원 m개 일반 위치 초평면 → 영역은 m^n/n! 차수. 의미: n차원 공간을 m개 평면으로 자르면 만들어지는 조각 개수의 정확한 공식. 평면 수가 많을수록 차원 수의 지수로 조각이 늘어남. ReLU 네트워크의 '표현력'이 깊이에 지수적이라는 주장의 수학적 뿌리. 참조: Zaslavsky Mem. AMS 154 (1975).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Hanin-Rolnick Bound (ReLU Network Region Count)
nodes.summary: Hanin & Rolnick 2019: 너비 n, 깊이 L인 ReLU 네트워크의 입력 공간 분할 영역 개수 R_{n,L}은 R_{n,L} ≤ C·(L·n/d)^d (d = 입력 차원, C = 상수). 결정적 상한 vs 평균 경계: 순수 Zaslavsky 경계는 layer 내 초평면을 일반 위치로 취급 → 최대 2^{nL} 영역 가능. Hanin-Rolnick의 기여는 훈련된 네트워크의 평균(초기화 분포 하) 영역 수가 다항식적으로 훨씬 작음을 증명 → O((Ln)^d). 유도: 가중치 iid Gaussian 가정 + Kac-Rice 공식으로 경계 교차 횟수 기댓값 + 조합 합성. State-Region Dictionary 연결: atlas cardinality = R_{n,L}, 각 region이 autoencoder의 한 state → dictionary 크기의 이론적 상한. Layer D Open Point 4에서 명시적으로 참조. 의미: ReLU 네트워크의 구역 사전이 실용적으로 유지/학습 가능한 다항 크기. 참조: arXiv:1906.00904; Serra et al. arXiv:1711.02114.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Switched Volterra Operator (PWL + Volterra)
nodes.summary: {P_q}를 ReLU/kink 유도 polytope 분할, 각 P_q 위에서 N이 affine A_q z+b_q라 하자. 입력→출력 사상: y(t) = F[u](t) = Σ_q 1_{u(·)∈P_q}·V^q[u](t), V^q는 P_q에서의 linear Volterra operator(사실상 LTI). Switching 처리: 입력 trajectory가 영역 경계를 건널 때 q(t)가 변하나 측도 0의 시점이라 적분 기여 0 → Lebesgue 적분 의미에서 무시 가능. 완결성: switched Volterra operator는 고전적 아날로그 신호처리의 표준 객체(DAC, threshold-based 회로, 아날로그 필터). 우리 NN 아키텍처는 이 가족 → '특별한 AI 하드웨어'가 아닌 확장된 고전 신호처리 장치. Layer D Theorem 3 Part (b)의 결론 자체이며, digitalization 우회의 정확한 수학 주장으로 환원: 입력-출력 사상 ∈ switched Volterra → analog primitive만으로 실현 가능 → 형식화→하드웨어 구현의 다리.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Geometry-Combinatorics-Analysis Triangle
nodes.summary: 세 도구의 삼각 연결성: (1) Piecewise-Linear System(구조 정의) ↔ Layer C(kink, f_kink), Layer D Theorem 3 Part (b); (2) Hyperplane Arrangement(조합 분석) ↔ Zaslavsky 정리(영역 개수), Hanin-Rolnick(실제 상한); (3) Switched Volterra Operator(신호처리 객체) ↔ Layer D Theorem 3 최종 결론, State-Region Dictionary 연결. 결합 효과: '깊이 있는 ReLU 네트워크는 유한 조각 선형 필터로 해석될 수 있다'는 정밀 주장이 완성. AMBIGUOUS Edge 1(Punch-Through ↔ Iterative Flow)과 Edge 2(Kink ↔ SRD) 모두 이 사슬의 직접 귀결로 해소. 필요 이유: (a) Theorem 3의 'finite 분할' 가정이 finite의 구체적 크기를 요구 → Zaslavsky+Hanin-Rolnick, (b) State-Region Dictionary 논문과의 무손실 대응 → hyperplane arrangement 언어로 번역, (c) Stage 2/3 simulation에서 region 수 예측 → SPICE 영역 전환 관측 예상 회수.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Math Theory 03 — Stochastic Calculus
nodes.description: Theory background note 3: stochastic calculus toolkit organized as the chain Brownian motion -> Ito integral -> SDE -> Fokker-Planck -> OU process, used to justify Layer S SDE system (S1)-(S3) and Theorems 4-5 for thermal noise, 1/f noise, and device variability.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Brownian Motion (Wiener Process)
nodes.description: Stochastic process B(t) with B(0)=0, independent increments, and B(t)-B(s) ~ N(0, t-s). Nowhere differentiable with quadratic variation [B,B]_t = t. Models thermal white noise; dB^x, dB^W, dB^delta in Layer S SDEs (S1)-(S3).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Ito Integral
nodes.description: Integral int f(t) dB(t) defined as L^2 limit of left-endpoint Riemann sums over adapted integrands, satisfying the Ito isometry E[(int f dB)^2] = E[int f^2 dt]. Non-anticipating (Ito) convention preserves martingale property, unlike Stratonovich.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Ito's Lemma (Stochastic Chain Rule)
nodes.description: For dX = mu dt + sigma dB and f in C^{1,2}: df = (df/dt) dt + (df/dx) dX + (1/2)(d^2 f/dx^2) sigma^2 dt. The extra curvature term from (dB)^2 = dt is the source of noise-induced bias (1/2 Tr(sigma^2 H_V)) in Theorem 4 Lyapunov analysis.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) Framework
nodes.description: dX = mu(t,X) dt + sigma(t,X) dB with strong existence/uniqueness under Lipschitz + linear growth (Ito 1951). Layer S (S3) dW = (1/tau_W)(-W + eta S deltaW) dt + sigma_W dB^W is an instance; Layer D is the sigma=0 special case.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Fokker-Planck Equation
nodes.description: PDE for the density p(t,x) of X(t): dp/dt = -d/dx(mu p) + (1/2) d^2/dx^2 (sigma^2 p), a.k.a. Kolmogorov forward equation. Derived by applying Ito's lemma to test functions and integrating by parts. Stationary solution (dp/dt = 0) gives long-run distribution (Gaussian for OU).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process
nodes.description: Linear-drift, constant-diffusion SDE dX = -theta(X - mu) dt + sigma dB. Mean-reverting with explicit solution; stationary distribution N(mu, sigma^2/(2 theta)). Canonical model for thermally fluctuating bias-clamped node; basis for Layer S Theorem 5 noise-tolerance bound.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Martingale Convergence Theorem
nodes.description: For uniformly integrable martingale M(t): M(t) -> M_infty almost surely as t -> infty (Doob 1953). Since int f dB is a martingale (Ito integral with adapted f), combining with eta_t -> 0 kills stochastic part a.s. in Theorem 4 Step 3, leaving only drift (gradient flow).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Lyapunov Equation (High-Dim OU Stationary Covariance)
nodes.description: For high-dimensional OU dX = -A X dt + Sigma_B dB (A positive definite), stationary covariance Sigma satisfies A Sigma + Sigma A^T = Sigma_B Sigma_B^T. Directly used in Theorem 5 proof step 2 to obtain Sigma = (sigma^2/(2 eta)) H^{-1}.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.name: Stochastic Calculus Connectivity Chain
nodes.description: Chain: Brownian motion -> Ito integral -> Ito lemma -> SDE -> {Fokker-Planck, OU/Lyapunov} -> Theorem 5 bound; Martingale convergence branches off to Theorem 4 almost-sure convergence. Captures why each tool is indispensable for Layer S's noise-tolerance statements.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theory Background 4 — Stochastic approximation and two-timescale theory (tool chain underpinning Layer S Theorem 4)
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theory background 4 — stochastic approximation and two-timescale theory (tool chain underpinning layer s theorem 4)

nodes.label: Robbins-Monro 1951 stochastic approximation: θ_{n+1} = θ_n − a_n Y(θ_n) converges to h(θ)=0 under Σ a_n=∞, Σ a_n²<∞
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: robbins-monro 1951 stochastic approximation: θ_{n+1} = θ_n − a_n y(θ_n) converges to h(θ)=0 under σ a_n=∞, σ a_n2<∞

nodes.label: Kushner-Yin ODE method (Ljung 1977, Kushner-Clark 1978): interpolated SA iterate converges to the ODE dθ/dt=h(θ); Lyapunov V↓ gives a.s. convergence (Thm 5.2.1)
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: kushner-yin ode method (ljung 1977, kushner-clark 1978): interpolated sa iterate converges to the ode dθ/dt=h(θ); lyapunov v↓ gives a.s. convergence (thm 5.2.1)

nodes.label: Borkar (2008) two-timescale SA: if b_n/a_n → 0, the slow variable sees the fast one as quasi-stationary; dw/dt = g(θ*(w), w) (Thm 6.1)
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: borkar (2008) two-timescale sa: if b_n/a_n → 0, the slow variable sees the fast one as quasi-stationary; dw/dt = g(θ*(w), w) (thm 6.1)

nodes.label: Martingale convergence applied to SA: M_n = Σ a_k ξ_k has Var(M_n) ≤ Σ a_k² M < ∞ ⇒ M_n converges a.s.; noise term vanishes asymptotically
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: martingale convergence applied to sa: m_n = σ a_k ξ_k has var(m_n) ≤ σ a_k2 m < ∞ ⇒ m_n converges a.s.; noise term vanishes asymptotically

nodes.label: Khasminskii stochastic Lyapunov theorem: ℒV = ∇V·μ + ½ Tr(σ² H_V) ≤ −c V ⇒ X(t) → X* a.s. (LaSalle deterministic analogue)
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: khasminskii stochastic lyapunov theorem: lv = ∇v·μ + 1⁄2 tr(σ2 h_v) ≤ −c v ⇒ x(t) → x* a.s. (lasalle deterministic analogue)

nodes.label: NES / REINFORCE score-function identity: ∇_θ E[f] = E[f · ∇_θ log p(x;θ)]; for p=𝒩(θ, σ²I), ∇log p = (x−θ)/σ² — forward-only gradient estimate
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: nes / reinforce score-function identity: ∇_θ e[f] = e[f · ∇_θ log p(x;θ)]; for p=n(θ, σ2i), ∇log p = (x−θ)/σ2 — forward-only gradient estimate

nodes.label: Natural gradient / Fisher perspective of NES: EGGROLL rank-r specialization ∇_W E[f(W+δW)] = E[f · δW]/σ² with 1/r variance reduction (Theorem 5 rank-bias)
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: natural gradient / fisher perspective of nes: eggroll rank-r specialization ∇_w e[f(w+δw)] = e[f · δw]/σ2 with 1/r variance reduction (theorem 5 rank-bias)

nodes.label: Connectivity chain: Robbins-Monro → Kushner-Yin ODE → Borkar two-timescale → Martingale+Itô Lyapunov → EGGROLL Theorem 4, with REINFORCE→NES→EGGROLL parallel branch
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: connectivity chain: robbins-monro → kushner-yin ode → borkar two-timescale → martingale+ito lyapunov → eggroll theorem 4, with reinforce→nes→eggroll parallel branch

nodes.label: Theory Background 5 — High-dim Gaussian Concentration & Rank Convergence
nodes.summary: Documents the mathematical foundation behind Layer S Theorem 5 (noise tolerance) and EGGROLL's O(r^-1) rank convergence. Covers high-dim Gaussian concentration, Gaussian annulus theorem, CLT + Edgeworth expansion, NTK kernel view, score function variance for rank-r estimators, and information geometry (Fisher, natural gradient).
nodes.author: ws
nodes.norm_label: theory background 5 — high-dim gaussian concentration & rank convergence

nodes.label: High-Dimensional Gaussian Concentration
nodes.summary: For X ~ N(0, I_d): E[||X||^2] = d, Var[||X||^2] = 2d, so relative std = sqrt(2/d) -> 0. High-dim Gaussian concentrates on a thin spherical shell of radius ~sqrt(d), not near the origin. In 10,000-dim weight space, Gaussian perturbations live almost entirely on the shell of radius ~100. Foundation for why ES perturbations efficiently explore high-dim parameter space. References: Vershynin (2018) Ch. 3; Ledoux (2001).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Gaussian Annulus Theorem (Vershynin, Ledoux)
nodes.summary: For X ~ N(0, I_d): P(|||X|| - sqrt(d)| > t) <= 2 exp(-c t^2) with absolute c > 0. Sub-Gaussian tail bound on deviation from sqrt(d). Derived via Borell 1975 Lipschitz concentration applied to f(x) = ||x|| (which is 1-Lipschitz) plus E[||X||] ~ sqrt(d). In Layer S, explains why noise propagation on d = nm parameter space contributes sqrt(d)-scale effective size (source of Tr(H^-1) dimension dependence). For EGGROLL: rank-r subspace covers sqrt(r) exploration range vs full sqrt(d); ratio r/d. References: Ledoux Ch. 1-2; Boucheron-Lugosi-Massart (2013).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: CLT and Edgeworth Expansion
nodes.summary: Classical CLT: (X_bar_n - mu)/(sigma/sqrt(n)) -> N(0,1). Edgeworth expansion refines CLT with finite-sample corrections via Hermite polynomials: F_n(x) = Phi(x) - phi(x)(kappa_3/(6 sqrt(n))) H_2(x) + O(1/n). Derived via log of characteristic function plus Fourier inversion (Feller Ch. 16). Crucial for EGGROLL's O(r^-1) bound: CLT alone gives O(r^-1/2); Edgeworth's 1/n term enables O(r^-1). For Layer S Theorem 5's 1/r rank-bias term. References: Feller Vol. II Ch. 16; Bhattacharya & Ranga Rao (1976).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) Kernel View
nodes.summary: Jacot-Gabriel-Hongler (2018): infinite-width NN training dynamics reduce to kernel regression. NTK: Theta(x, x') = <grad_theta f(x; theta_0), grad_theta f(x'; theta_0)>. In infinite-width limit Theta is deterministic and df/dt = -Theta(x, X_train)(f - y). Connects to our formulation: Theorem 4 Step 1's averaged slow SDE is a stochastic variant of NTK flow with device noise. NTK contributes (1) convergence rate prediction via exponential rates; (2) lazy-training justification for Theorem 5's linearization around W*; (3) automatic universal approximation via RKHS density. References: Jacot et al. (NeurIPS 2018, arXiv:1806.07572); Lee et al. (NeurIPS 2019); Arora et al. (NeurIPS 2019).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Score Function Variance & Rank-r Extension
nodes.summary: Score estimator g = f . grad log p has Var[g] = E[f^2 ||grad log p||^2] - (E[g])^2. For high-dim Gaussian, ||grad log p|| = ||x - theta||/sigma^2 ~ sqrt(d)/sigma^2, so rank-1 estimator variance grows linearly in d. Rank-r average: g_avg = (1/r) sum f_k . dW_k/sigma^2, Var[g_avg] = Var[g]/r, so RMSE decays as 1/sqrt(r); Edgeworth correction drops bias to 1/r. EGGROLL's contributions: (1) structured single rank-r matrix for arithmetic intensity; (2) explicit Edgeworth-based O(r^-1) bound; (3) hyperscale numerical validation. Layer S Theorem 5 extends to device-noise case: bias <= C_H sigma_xi / r.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Information Geometry & Fisher Information
nodes.summary: Fisher information matrix I_ij(theta) = E[d_i log p . d_j log p]. Natural gradient I(theta)^-1 . grad respects intrinsic parameter-space geometry (Wierstra 2014 NES). Our formulation uses this implicitly: Theorem 5's H^-1 (Hessian inverse) is analogous to Fisher inverse — natural gradient adjustment happens automatically. Gaussian perturbation of W with sigma^2 covariance = isometric search under Fisher metric. Explains why ES/NES class algorithms work well; our formulation ports this principle to device physics. References: Amari (2016) Springer; Martens (2020) JMLR arXiv:1412.1193.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Super-Summary — Five Theory Notes Unified
nodes.summary: Binds all five theory-background notes into a unified foundation. (1) ODE Foundations (Picard, Lipschitz, LTI, Volterra) supplies Layer D. (2) Piecewise Systems (Zaslavsky, Hanin-Rolnick, switched Volterra) supplies Layer D Theorem 3 Part (b). (3) Stochastic Calculus (Brownian, Ito, Fokker-Planck, OU) provides Layer S SDE skeleton. (4) Stochastic Approximation (Robbins-Monro, Borkar, Lyapunov, martingale) supplies Layer S Theorem 4. (5) Gaussian Concentration (annulus, Edgeworth, NTK, info geometry) supplies Layer S Theorem 5 + EGGROLL convergence rate. The five requirements: (i) physics is a well-defined mathematical system (Layer C + Theory 1); (ii) system is classical signal-processing object (Layer D + Theory 1, 2); (iii) math still holds with noise (Layer S + Theory 3); (iv) system learns (Layer S + Theory 4); (v) learning maps to actual device parameters (Layer S + Theory 5). Each note carries at least one proof step that no other note can supply — removing any one breaks a Layer D/S theorem. This asserts the five theory notes form the complete unified foundation for the bit-analog formulation.
